title: Begin with an outcome-first product brief
principle: State the user, their job, the business outcome, the primary action, and the proof of success before choosing a visual treatment.
rationale: A concrete outcome prevents the interface from becoming a collection of fashionable sections with no decision hierarchy.
applies_when: Starting any new page or materially changing an existing flow.
exceptions: A narrowly scoped visual token correction may inherit the existing brief.
implementation: Write a five-line brief covering audience, job, outcome, primary action, and measurable success.
Record known constraints and unresolved assumptions separately.
verification: A reviewer can identify the primary user and action without seeing a mockup.
Every major section supports the stated job or is removed.
accessibility: Include disabled, assistive-technology, and reduced-motion users in audience assumptions.
responsive: Name which user jobs must remain possible at narrow and wide viewports.
performance: Add a page-level performance expectation when speed affects the outcome.
browser_notes: Capture the supported browser baseline as a requirement, not an implementation afterthought.

title: Separate requirements from preferences
principle: Classify inputs as required, strongly preferred, optional, or hypothesis, and make conflicts visible.
rationale: Unranked requests produce crowded pages and make subjective stakeholder preferences indistinguishable from legal or user needs.
applies_when: Multiple stakeholders, reference artifacts, or legacy constraints influence the work.
exceptions: None when requirements conflict; conflicts must be surfaced.
implementation: Create a requirement ledger with owner, priority, evidence, and acceptance check.
Resolve or explicitly defer contradictions before detailed polish.
verification: Each mandatory requirement maps to a testable acceptance check.
Optional ideas can be removed without breaking the promised outcome.
accessibility: Treat conformance and equivalent task completion as requirements, never preferences.
responsive: Record viewport-specific behavior only where the task truly changes.
performance: Budget requirements outrank decorative media preferences.
browser_notes: Support commitments identify browser versions or a documented baseline.

title: Demand evidence before adding a feature
principle: Add interface capability only when it serves observed behavior, a validated risk, or a measurable product goal.
rationale: Speculative features increase cognitive load, implementation cost, and states that must be made accessible and reliable.
applies_when: A proposed control or section has no direct acceptance criterion.
exceptions: A low-cost reversible experiment may ship when it has a named hypothesis and removal date.
implementation: Attach evidence or a hypothesis to each net-new capability.
Prefer the smallest test that answers the uncertainty.
verification: Every new feature has an owner, success signal, and rollback condition.
accessibility: Count the accessibility cost of every additional interaction state.
responsive: Confirm the feature remains usable without consuming the narrow viewport's primary task space.
performance: Estimate code, request, and media cost before accepting the feature.
browser_notes: Avoid novelty that relies on unsupported platform behavior without a fallback.

title: Design with representative data and failure cases
principle: Use representative short, long, empty, missing, delayed, malformed, and localized values before approving a layout.
rationale: Idealized placeholder data conceals truncation, ambiguity, empty states, and error recovery until late implementation.
applies_when: The interface renders user, API, CMS, or translated content.
exceptions: Purely decorative prototypes may use synthetic data if explicitly labeled non-production.
implementation: Build a fixture set that covers common and boundary values.
Model loading, partial, empty, error, and permission-denied responses.
verification: Run visual and interaction checks against every fixture class.
No required meaning exists only in clipped text or hover content.
accessibility: Expose errors, empty results, and loading status programmatically.
responsive: Test the longest representative values at the narrowest supported width.
performance: Use realistic collection sizes to reveal rendering or virtualization needs.
browser_notes: Test native wrapping, hyphenation, and input behavior in the browser matrix.

title: Give each page one dominant task
principle: Express a clear primary task and subordinate secondary actions through order, copy, and visual emphasis.
rationale: When every action is prominent, users must reconstruct priority and conversion or completion suffers.
applies_when: A page asks the user to choose or act.
exceptions: A utility hub may support several peer tasks, but their categories must still be explicit.
implementation: Name the primary task in a verb phrase.
Limit primary visual emphasis to the action that advances that task.
verification: A five-second review consistently identifies the intended next action.
Removing secondary actions does not obscure the page purpose.
accessibility: DOM order, heading order, and accessible names reflect the same hierarchy.
responsive: Keep the primary task visible before secondary content on narrow screens.
performance: Prioritize resources needed for the primary task.
browser_notes: Do not depend on hover to reveal the primary action.

title: Reveal complexity at the moment it becomes useful
principle: Keep essential choices visible and defer advanced or conditional controls behind explicit, reversible disclosure.
rationale: Progressive disclosure reduces initial decision cost without removing access to expert capability.
applies_when: A minority of users need advanced settings or conditional details.
exceptions: Do not hide safety, price, eligibility, validation, or irreversible consequences.
implementation: Use a descriptive disclosure label that predicts the revealed content.
Preserve user input when sections open or close.
verification: Core completion is possible without opening advanced controls.
Expanded state is keyboard operable and announced.
accessibility: Use native disclosure semantics or an APG-conformant pattern with expanded state.
responsive: Ensure expanded content does not create off-screen controls or nested scrolling.
performance: Lazy-load only genuinely heavy optional content and provide a stable loading state.
browser_notes: Native details/summary behavior should be verified against the support baseline before customization.

title: Front-load meaning in labels and headings
principle: Put the differentiating noun or action early; use specific labels that remain meaningful out of visual context.
rationale: Users scan edges and assistive technology may present links or controls as lists, so vague endings and repeated labels slow recognition.
applies_when: Writing headings, navigation labels, buttons, links, field labels, and error summaries.
exceptions: A familiar compact command may remain short when surrounding context is programmatically associated.
implementation: Prefer labels such as 'Download invoice' over 'Click here'.
Remove throat-clearing words from headings.
verification: Control purpose is clear when read alone.
Heading and link lists remain distinguishable.
accessibility: Accessible names match or include visible labels and communicate destination or action.
responsive: Do not solve narrow widths by removing the distinguishing words.
browser_notes: Text remains selectable and is not replaced by canvas-rendered labels.

title: Write errors as recovery instructions
principle: State what happened, what the user can do next, and whether their work is preserved without blame or internal codes.
rationale: Errors are high-stress decision points; diagnostic-only copy leaves the user stranded and erodes trust.
applies_when: Validation, network, permission, conflict, or server failures block or alter a task.
exceptions: Technical detail may appear in an optional support disclosure with a copyable incident identifier.
implementation: Place field-specific guidance next to the field and summarize submission failures at the form start.
Offer a safe retry, alternate route, or contact path.
verification: A user can name the next step from the message alone.
Focus moves predictably to an error summary or first invalid field when appropriate.
accessibility: Associate inline errors to inputs and announce new blocking messages without stealing focus unexpectedly.
responsive: Errors wrap without covering inputs or actions.
performance: Retry behavior is bounded and does not create request storms.
browser_notes: Custom validation does not suppress useful native semantics unless fully replaced.

title: Make every line earn its place
principle: Use the smallest amount of copy that preserves the task, decision, trust, and recovery information; remove lines or sections that repeat a message or do not change what the user understands or does.
rationale: Redundant explanation slows scanning, weakens hierarchy, lengthens responsive pages, and competes with the few words and actions that actually move the user forward.
applies_when: Writing or reviewing page-level marketing, onboarding, product, dashboard, or instructional content.
exceptions: Keep safety, legal, price, eligibility, consent, validation, recovery, and accessibility instructions when omission would increase risk or ambiguity.
Long-form editorial content may be intentionally substantial when reading is the primary task.
implementation: Give every content block one decision or task job, keep the strongest occurrence of repeated meaning, and delete the rest.
Put essential context next to the action it affects and move genuinely optional depth behind a descriptive, reversible disclosure.
verification: A five-second review identifies the page purpose, primary action, and necessary trust or consequence information.
Remove each sentence or section in turn; if task completion, decision quality, trust, or recovery does not degrade, leave it out.
accessibility: Do not replace necessary text with unexplained icons, color, motion, hover-only help, or visual position.
Keep instructions and consequences available in reading order before the action they affect.
responsive: Reduce semantic redundancy instead of truncating essential words or hiding required information only at narrow widths.
performance: Fewer nonessential sections can reduce DOM, media, and interaction cost; verify the actual build rather than claiming a performance gain from copy reduction alone.
browser_notes: Keep essential copy as selectable semantic text and verify disclosures with keyboard, focus, and expanded-state behavior.

title: Keep build narration out of product copy
principle: State user-relevant availability, limits, and consequences in product language; do not fill customer-facing pages with narration about prompts, supplied facts, mocks, test fixtures, missing backends, unimplemented endpoints, agent honesty, or why unsupported claims were omitted.
rationale: Internal QA narration competes with the value proposition, makes prototypes sound defensive, and turns integrity work into repetitive disclaimer copy instead of better scope, controls, and information architecture.
applies_when: A public, customer-facing, portfolio, or product surface mentions development status, mocked data, source availability, implementation gaps, fictional fixtures, or unsupported claims.
exceptions: Keep a concise boundary when it changes whether an action is safe, real, available, paid, legally effective, or externally transmitted.
Internal test harnesses, developer documentation, status pages, and release-readiness views may expose implementation detail because that detail is the user's task.
implementation: Keep fact provenance, unsupported-claim notes, and build limitations in the internal content ledger, project documentation, or completion report rather than turning them into marketing sections.
When a capability is unavailable, omit the promise and dead action; if the remaining control is a local preview, name it once at the point of action and state the consequence plainly.

Replace statements such as 'no metric was supplied,' 'this is only a mocked connector,' or 'the backend is not implemented' with verified product evidence, a concise availability label, a useful next action, or no section at all.
verification: Read the visible page without styling and flag every sentence about the build, generator, prompt, fixture, provenance process, or absent system; retain only text that changes a user decision, consequence, trust judgment, or recovery path.
No section exists mainly to announce that the implementation avoided fabrication.
Required scope boundaries appear once, adjacent to the affected action, in language the user can act on.
accessibility: Do not hide necessary consent, price, eligibility, safety, transmission, validation, or availability information while removing internal narration.
responsive: Keep required boundary text adjacent to its action and readable without duplicating it in hero, body, and footer.
performance: Removing meta-copy may reduce page length and DOM size, but do not claim a performance improvement without measurement.
browser_notes: A disabled or preview-only control exposes its actual state and consequence programmatically; explanatory prose does not compensate for a misleading control.

title: Keep action vocabulary continuous through the flow
principle: Name the same user action with the same recognizable verb and object across its trigger, confirmation, progress, success, failure, navigation, and recovery states.
rationale: Vocabulary drift makes users re-interpret whether a control, status, and result belong to the same operation, weakening learnability and increasing uncertainty at consequential steps.
applies_when: An action spans more than one component, route, asynchronous state, or confirmation step.
exceptions: A destructive confirmation may expand the label to state the irreversible consequence, but it should preserve the original action term and target.
implementation: Create a small interface vocabulary for core nouns, verbs, states, and outcomes; prefer user-recognizable language over internal system terms.
Audit each flow from visible trigger through completion and recovery so action labels predict the next state and status copy uses the same terminology.
verification: Read the trigger, pending state, confirmation, success, error, and recovery copy out of context; each remains specific and clearly refers to one operation.
Accessible names and visible labels use the same core terms, and no generic Submit, Continue, Done, or technical implementation label obscures the consequence.
accessibility: Visible and programmatic names stay aligned; status announcements identify the affected action and outcome without relying on position or color.
responsive: Narrow layouts wrap or recompose labels without replacing the distinguishing verb or object with an ambiguous icon or shorter generic word.
performance: No material performance impact; centralized copy catalogs should not delay rendering of critical controls or status.
browser_notes: Native form and validation language is overridden only when the replacement preserves semantics, localization, and complete recovery guidance.
